Yeah. Well, I mean, it's not, it's a good way. It's not the only way, but I mean, certainly going over there and doing an event over there and selling them into this program is a good way. The, if you do it, the one piece of advice that I have for you about that is, is, it's got to be sort of like the secret discovered in Australia is now the hellable to the people in the UK because everybody sort of likes to buy the secret. You know, when, when I went over to Australia and spoke over there, it was like a big thing that people from the United States were coming over there. You know, we have four speakers that were coming over there. So it's got to be a big thing that people are coming from the, you know, this is something that, you know, you got to really be building on how, how successful this is in the, in Australia. And now this is now available to the UK. The other model that probably would work as well and, and may be less painful to you considering the fact that it sounds like you don't want to do the speaking is, is to develop really a UK partner that now you can build the success around his story. So find somebody over, for lack of a better terminology, find somebody over in the UK that sort of becomes like your super franchise for the UK. And now, you know, and now, you know, he's the person that's responsible to be providing all these area franchises over there or area licenses or however you decide to structure that. And now you sort of build the story on his back where now he's discovered you and it would be best if he actually does it himself and he has great success to, to share. So you're sort of looking for a business partner over there, but also has a great story to tell on his success by using what he's learned from you and applying it to the UK market. Now let him do all the grunt work of going out and selling them as well. So if I do that, and that's one of the things I've had to discuss, what percentage of the action do I give him? Because I've got to go out in the UK. How much of the dollars do I give him and how much do I give? Well, that's a discussion. That's an impossible answer for me to give you without knowing all the economics and finances of your business. But my short answer is as little as you have to. But I don't know how much, you know, what's there, you know? So I don't know how much money is there. Okay? I have two questions. One of them is about authority, the whole issue of authority. You mentioned that people may go off and splinter off once they get to know each other. How have you dealt with that or have you had to deal with that? Because it's a legitimate issue. Well, I never let it get to that point. I mean, you have to, when you're running, when you're in a group meeting, a mastermind setting, you have to still make sure that you're in total control of that setting. And you just can never let it get to the point where you are no longer in control of that setting. So, for example, you have to have a very definite, this is what's going to go on here. Here's the program that's happening here and we're sticking to the program. And because of that, because you have a program and you've developed the program, that in and of itself is a strong reason for people to be fearful of leaving you anyway. Because you're the one that developed the program and now every time Bill comes or every time Marlene comes, she's got something that she's bringing that only Marlene has. So, if you're answering the question specifically to your mastermind groups in your IBA area, you have two things. One is you've got the Dan Kennedy story and the Dan Kennedy DVDs and the Dan Kennedy program for that day. And then you also should be bringing something that Marlene brings to the table, a specific something that you want to discuss, maybe a specific success that you've encountered and you want to teach that to other people or something that you've learned here that you want to teach somebody else. Each time there's something that only Marlene's program and nobody else could have this. Well, actually it was actually a general question because I know people drop out of masterminds in general as to they built a relationship with the other people in the group and they feel I don't need to pay for that anymore. So is that really a big problem or not really? It really isn't. It's an interesting, it's a good question by the way. And my judgment, it's actually, it's not only not a big problem but it's a good thing. I really think that if every year there's about a 20% churn in your mastermind group, it brings a lot of new life and vitality to the group. I've been a member of the Platinum Group now for this is my ninth year and I've always enjoyed when we had new members into the group. In my own coaching groups, I love it when we get new people in every year. You don't want to have too many new people because the groups form a real bond and relationship with each other. But about a 20% churn is very, very good for the groups. Okay, great. And my second question is regarding... That was two questions. This is your third question. This is a bonus question. This is a bonus question. Your bonus question is, in general, in the mastermind groups, what kind of problems do you... The number one type of problem? Is it more technical stuff or is it organizational stuff or marketing? Yes. All of it. But the number one thing, this was the number one thing that you find over and over because I find when I'm talking to people in the group, it's not so much the marketing, it's organizational issues that they're dealing with. That's what comes out eventually. I still go back to my original answer. Yes. I think I actually see it's all of them. They have marketing questions, they have organization questions, time management, getting things done. Those of you in the room that are in part some of my group, when you sort of agree with that, we just sort of go through them all and depends on the issues, which is a reason why, by the way, that diversity in the group is really good. Which is another reason why, by the way, I believe that if you're in a... Now this is from a different perspective, not selling it but being a member of, you want to be a member of a group that's in your niche and you want to be a member of a group that's outside your niche. Because when you sell in the groups, that's one of the... If it's not a niche group, that's one of the real strengths to sell it as is you have other people in there that experience other things in other industries. But yeah, all three of the things that you asked. Things like getting things done and time management, that's something that's not necessarily niche specific.
